summary: Gruber examines the confusion surrounding Apple's new App Store subscription pricing rules announced in 2016. While Phil Schiller suggested that professional apps and productivity tools could adopt subscription models, Apple's official documentation narrowly defines eligible apps as 'Content' or 'Services,' creating uncertainty for developers. Gruber argues Apple should allow any app to offer subscriptions and let the market decide, rather than maintaining vague gatekeeping rules. He also explores the tradeoff between subscriptions and traditional paid upgrades, noting that subscriptions remove users' ability to keep using older versions they've already paid for. He acknowledges subscriptions aren't a panacea but sees them as the inevitable direction given Apple's refusal to implement paid upgrade pricing.
key_points: Apple's written subscription rules contradict what Schiller verbally communicated to press, leaving developers unsure whether productivity and professional apps qualify
The vague 'I know it when I see it' approach to subscription eligibility forces developers to do all engineering work before learning if they'll be approved
Apple's rules create perverse incentives: apps with proprietary sync can subscribe but CloudKit-based apps cannot, despite Apple wanting developers to use iCloud
Subscription pricing eliminates users' freedom to skip upgrades while keeping their current version, unlike traditional paid-upgrade models
The software industry is broadly moving toward subscriptions regardless, led by Adobe and Microsoft, making this shift inevitable
money_quotes: Like Justice Potter Stewart's 'I know it when I see it' definition of 'obscenity', I think Apple wants to define 'good use of the subscription business model' as 'we know it when we see it'.
This uncertainty is downright untenable for a new app whose developer sees subscription pricing as the only sustainable business model to justify the app's development in the first place.
An app with its own proprietary sync service can use the subscription model, but a competing app that provides the same features using CloudKit cannot. But Apple wants developers to use iCloud.
I think Apple should just allow any app to offer subscription pricing, period.

Apple's role should be as the trusted platform vendor, making sure users can easily cancel subscriptions, requiring opt-in to any pricing changes, and making sure no one is being tricked or confused in any way.
People won't sign up for a bad subscription offering for the same reasons they don't sign up for bad subscription deals in the world outside the App Store.
I don't think subscription pricing — even if Apple clarified that subscriptions are open to any app, period — are a panacea. There is no perfect way to sell software.
key_insight: Apple's vague subscription eligibility rules create paralyzing uncertainty for developers who need to know whether their business model will be approved before investing in building it.
